title: Threads
description: Covers threads, multithreading models, and Amdahl's law.

questions.prompt: What is the name for a unit of CPU execution that shares much of its address space (code, data, heap) with its siblings?
Which law gives an upper bound on the speedup obtainable by parallelising a process?
Which of the following are shared between threads of the same process?
Match each user-to-kernel thread mapping to its property.
right: Each user thread maps to a kernel thread; thread creation is expensive but parallelism is easy
Programmer chooses how many kernel threads back the user threads; flexible and supports parallelism
Many user threads share a single kernel thread; cheap but cannot run in parallel and blocks on one thread block the lot

questions.prompt: Which of the following are advantages of a thread pool over thread-per-request?
options: Less overhead since threads are pre-created
Better responsiveness when load is high (up to a point)
Eliminates all need to queue incoming requests
Easier to reason about resource limits
Guarantees requests are served in arrival order

questions.prompt: Which term means that more than one task can make progress, but not necessarily simultaneously?
Which term means processing actually done simultaneously, typically on multiple cores?
Which of the following are TRUE about signal handling?
options: Synchronous signals are generated by the running process
Asynchronous signals are externally generated (for example, by the user)
A user-defined handler can override the default handler
Signals can be delivered to a specific thread or to all threads
SIGINT should always be delivered to only one specific thread
